does: This paper trains a two-layer transformer encoder to classify elliptic curves as rank 0 or rank 1 using Frobenius traces, achieving >99% accuracy on curves with conductor ≤10000. The authors then apply mechanistic interpretability techniques including attention analysis, activation patching, and neuron-level circuit analysis to reverse-engineer the learned algorithm. They discover that a sparse circuit of only 20 out of 512 layer-1 MLP neurons suffices for rank prediction, implementing a push-pull detector architecture with rank-0 and rank-1 detectors connected through one-sided readout, achieving an AUROC of 0.992 at plateau.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of machine learning and number theory, extending prior studies using neural networks to predict arithmetic properties of elliptic curves. It responds to recent advances in mechanistic interpretability that have successfully reverse-engineered algorithms in transformer models trained on synthetic and natural tasks. The paper directly engages with the classical Mestre-Nagao heuristic from computational number theory, suggesting the transformer has rediscovered or approximated this mathematical technique. It combines circuit analysis methods from interpretability research with domain knowledge from arithmetic geometry to understand what a neural network learns about curve properties.
enables: This work opens several directions for future research at the intersection of AI and mathematics. It demonstrates that mechanistic interpretability can uncover how neural networks learn to implement mathematical heuristics, potentially enabling discovery of new computational techniques in number theory. The sparse circuit discovery suggests that interpretability can identify the minimal algorithmic components needed for complex mathematical tasks. This approach could extend to other elliptic curve properties (different rank classes, torsion structure) or broader arithmetic geometry problems. Understanding neural solutions to mathematical problems may also inform the design of better classical algorithms and provide new perspectives on why certain heuristics work in practice.
